posts.name: Chris V a n d e r m a r e l
posts.cooked:
In thee thread linked below I realized I had been confusing JJ VIncent and JA Ferret in Pouilly-Fuissé and William Kelley set me straight. And man was he right! Really lovely bottle of white burgundy here form JA Ferret. I poured it next to a de Montille C-M Villages for a baseline comparison (the Montille was also recently discounted here to the same price as the JA Ferret).

WineBerserkers – 7 Jul 20

2017 J.A. Ferret Pouilly-Fuissé

Delicious and refreshing. Wet stone, yellow apple, and white peach greet you on the nose. Elegant, the palate is pear and crisp lemon, the fruit sun-kissed without trending towards the tropical and the profile is generous and round without being...

Reading time: 3 mins 🕑 Likes: 2 ❤

  • 2017 Domaine de Montille Chassagne-Montrachet - France, Burgundy, Côte de Beaune, Chassagne-Montrachet (12/11/2020)
    Followed over 24 hours. Pours medium straw. The light and airy nose shows white flowers, cold steel, crushed rock, dry hay, cold ceam, pear, a touch of youthful reduction via a slight struck match note, and a touch of custard. The palate is light and elegant with high acid and a somewhat sour finish, which lingers on a candied mint note. Could almost be confused with a Chablis. It’s quite taught and mineral with high acid and very little noticeable oak. A really enjoyable and lovely bottle here from de Montille. Almost 92. (91 pts.)


  • 2017 J.A. Ferret Pouilly-Fuissé Clos des Prouges Tête de Cru - France, Burgundy, Mâconnais, Pouilly-Fuissé (12/11/2020)
    Followed over 24 hours. Pours medium straw. The nose is already lifted and complex. White mushroom, touches of struck flint, candied lemon peel, custard, green apple, churned earth, almond, and chalk round out the gorgeous aromatic profile. The palate has pitch perfect balance of leesy richness and linear, tight acid (medium plus). The finish lingers impressively showing lime zest, custard, and candied lemon peel. Thoroughly enjoyed this wine. William Kelley got it right! (93 pts.)

posts.name: Kris_P@tten
posts.cooked:
…and that is just the regular Pouilly Fuissé. Try the Tetê de Cru tier.

I’d say a pro just confirmed what previous amateurs said in that thread.

William is a very gifted gentleman though and generous with his time and opinion.

Now I’m curious to try these again.

posts.display_username: Chris V a n d e r m a r e l

posts.name: Kris_P@tten
posts.cooked:
…and that is just the regular Pouilly Fuissé. Try the Tetê de Cru tier.

I’d say a pro just confirmed what previous amateurs said in that thread.

William is a very gifted gentleman though and generous with his time and opinion.

D’oh! It was the Clos des Prouges Tête de Cru. Grabbed the wrong wine in CT. Just corrected my OP.

Regardless of the wine, there is world class Chardonnay produced outside of regions that have Grand Cru classifications. The only downside is as they are discovered prices will rise.

William is certainly a great voice for Macconais and the farmers that toil there and the vignerons who farm and toil to make singularly interesting and thought provoking wines. Just don’t speak too loudly til our cellars are full William!

Makes it fun to taste and explore in Macon vs. chase trophies and hunt for deals in the Cote de Beaune and Chablis.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
…and that is just the regular Pouilly Fuissé. Try the Tetê de Cru tier.

I’d say a pro just confirmed what previous amateurs said in that thread.

William is a very gifted gentleman though and generous with his time and opinion.

D’oh! It was the Clos des Prouges Tête de Cru. Grabbed the wrong wine in CT. Just corrected my OP.

Regardless of the wine, there is world class Chardonnay produced outside of regions that have Grand Cru classifications. The only downside is as they are discovered prices will rise.

William is certainly a great voice for Macconais and the farmers that toil there and the vignerons who farm and toil to make singularly interesting and thought provoking wines. Just don’t speak too loudly til our cellars are full William!

Makes it fun to taste and explore in Macon vs. chase trophies and hunt for deals in the Cote de Beaune and Chablis.

Thanks, everyone!

I certainly believe in the potential of the Mâconnais. And, even if we might not like it, I think it’s important to be prepared to pay for it… One sad tendency I have seen in the Mâconnais and other less prestigious regions (e.g. Montagny, Mercurey, Rully etc) is for young winemakers to start out doing everything necessary for high quality; they are celebrated for it, yes, but their scores rapidly hit a glass ceiling and importers are mostly only prepared to pay so much for Mâcon—if prices rise, they can get the same AOCs for a lot less elsewhere. So, these producers who started with such promise let their yields creep up and pay a bit less attention - the wines are still good, but less ambitious - and the scores etc remain unchanged. If we want true excellence in the Mâconnais, on the one hand, the press needs to be prepared to score the wines the way they would if they were tasting them blind, in the company of high appellation Côte de Beaune whites; and on the other, consumers have to support excellence with their wallets.

Anyway, really glad you appreciated the bottle. Different in style to the rich, unctuous Ferret wines of yesteryear (have a bunch of 1983s in my cellar, thanks to this forum’s Eric Egan), but for me some of the best Pouilly-Fuissé being made today.

posts.name: Kris_P@tten
posts.cooked:

Regardless of the wine, there is world class Chardonnay produced outside of regions that have Grand Cru classifications. The only downside is as they are discovered prices will rise.

William is certainly a great voice for Macconais and the farmers that toil there and the vignerons who farm and toil to make singularly interesting and thought provoking wines. Just don’t speak too loudly til our cellars are full William!

Makes it fun to taste and explore in Macon vs. chase trophies and hunt for deals in the Cote de Beaune and Chablis.

Thanks, everyone!

I certainly believe in the potential of the Mâconnais. And, even if we might not like it, I think it’s important to be prepared to pay for it… One sad tendency I have seen in the Mâconnais and other less prestigious regions (e.g. Montagny, Mercurey, Rully etc) is for young winemakers to start out doing everything necessary for high quality; they are celebrated for it, yes, but their scores rapidly hit a glass ceiling and importers are mostly only prepared to pay so much for Mâcon—if prices rise, they can get the same AOCs for a lot less elsewhere. So, these producers who started with such promise let their yields creep up and pay a bit less attention - the wines are still good, but less ambitious - and the scores etc remain unchanged. If we want true excellence in the Mâconnais, on the one hand, the press needs to be prepared to score the wines the way they would if they were tasting them blind, in the company of high appellation Côte de Beaune whites; and on the other, consumers have to support excellence with their wallets.

Anyway, really glad you appreciated the bottle. Different in style to the rich, unctuous Ferret wines of yesteryear (have a bunch of 1983s in my cellar, thanks to this forum’s Eric Egan), but for me some of the best Pouilly-Fuissé being made today.

Agreed, but look at the trajectory of Saint Aubin, less than a decade ago you could get world class Chardonnay for under $50, as the region became “found” prices went over $100 in many cases and there exists a ceiling for consumers willingness to spend their dollars or find the next region that is within their budget.

It doesn’t change that PYCM or Lamy make world class wines, they just lose a portion of their consumer base, which is unfortunately, is usually the same group that got them there, to be replaced by those with more expendable income.

Happens in every wine region, what I always hope is it makes it into the winemakers pocket, not the importer.

It’s a possibility. But St. Aubin is a stone’s throw from the Montrachet GCs. Vergison and Fuisse are not.
